hypothesis: If single-step retrosynthesis models are trained on datasets with skewed template frequencies and restricted template diversity, then they will show inflated top-k and round-trip accuracies on in-distribution and seen-template (Pistachio-ID) reactions but markedly lower accuracies on unseen-template (Pistachio-OOD) reactions, with performance positively correlating with template frequency and train–test Tanimoto similarity, because the models exploit dataset biases and memorize frequent transformations rather than learning generalizable disconnection rules.
